**Martin Heidegger**[[a]](#cite_note-4) (26 September 1889 – 26 May 1976) was a German philosopher whose work was central to the development of [phenomenology](/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)"), [hermeneutics](/wiki/Hermeneutics "Hermeneutics"), and [existentialism](/wiki/Existentialism "Existentialism"). He has had a considerable impact on fields ranging from [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") and [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology") to [literary theory](/wiki/Literary_theory "Literary theory") and [political philosophy](/wiki/Political_philosophy "Political philosophy").

Heidegger's [magnum opus](/wiki/Magnum_opus "Magnum opus"), *[Being and Time](/wiki/Being_and_Time "Being and Time")* (1927), is widely considered one of the most significant works of [modern philosophy](/wiki/Modern_philosophy "Modern philosophy").[[4]](#cite_note-5) In it, he introduced the concept of *[Dasein](/wiki/Dasein "Dasein")* ("being-there") to describe the distinctive character of human existence, arguing that humans possess a "pre-[ontological](/wiki/Ontology "Ontology")" understanding of being that shapes how they live and act, which he analyzed in terms of the unitary structure of "being-in-the-world". Through his analysis of *Dasein*, Heidegger sought to reawaken what he called "the question of [being](/wiki/Existence "Existence")": the fundamental inquiry into what makes entities intelligible as the entities they are. In other words, Heidegger's governing "question of being" is concerned with what makes beings intelligible as beings. This question, he believed, had been neglected or obscured throughout the history of [Western philosophy](/wiki/Western_philosophy "Western philosophy") since the [ancient Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_philosophy "Ancient Greek philosophy").

His later work turned increasingly to questions of [technology](/wiki/The_Question_Concerning_Technology "The Question Concerning Technology"), language, art, and poetry, developing themes of human "dwelling" in the world and critiquing what he saw as the [nihilistic](/wiki/Nihilism "Nihilism") trajectory of modern technological civilisation. Thinkers as varied as [Jean-Paul Sartre](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sartre "Jean-Paul Sartre"), [Hans-Georg Gadamer](/wiki/Hans-Georg_Gadamer "Hans-Georg Gadamer"), [Hannah Arendt](/wiki/Hannah_Arendt "Hannah Arendt"), [Jacques Derrida](/wiki/Jacques_Derrida "Jacques Derrida"), and [Richard Rorty](/wiki/Richard_Rorty "Richard Rorty") were substantially shaped by engagement with his thought, whether in agreement or opposition.

Heidegger's legacy has been complicated and shadowed by his involvement with [National Socialism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism"). In April 1933, he was elected [rector](/wiki/Rector_(academia) "Rector (academia)") of the [University of Freiburg](/wiki/University_of_Freiburg "University of Freiburg") and joined the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party"), a membership he retained until 1945. The nature and extent of his commitment to Nazism, and the question of whether his philosophy is inherently connected to his political choices, remain [subjects](/wiki/Martin_Heidegger_and_Nazism "Martin Heidegger and Nazism") of significant scholarly controversy. After the war, he was banned from teaching following [denazification](/wiki/Denazification "Denazification") proceedings, a ban later lifted in 1949, after which he returned to lecturing at the University of Freiburg. During this period of enforced withdrawal, he nevertheless continued to exert influence through private seminars and audiences at his home. His refusal to publicly repudiate his Nazi involvements or express remorse in unambiguous terms has continued to trouble interpreters of his work.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Geburtshaus_Heidegger_Sonne.JPG/250px-Geburtshaus_Heidegger_Sonne.JPG)](/wiki/File:Geburtshaus_Heidegger_Sonne.JPG)

The Mesnerhaus in [Meßkirch](/wiki/Me%C3%9Fkirch "Meßkirch"), where Heidegger grew up

Heidegger was born on 26 September 1889 in rural [Meßkirch](/wiki/Me%C3%9Fkirch "Meßkirch"), [Baden](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Baden "Grand Duchy of Baden"), the son of Johanna (Kempf) and Friedrich Heidegger. His father was the [sexton](/wiki/Sexton_(office) "Sexton (office)") of the village church, and the young Martin was raised [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2019chapter_1-6)

In 1903, Heidegger began to train for the [priesthood](/wiki/Priesthood "Priesthood"). He entered a [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") [seminary](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary") in 1909, but was discharged within weeks because of heart trouble. It was during this time that he first encountered the work of [Franz Brentano](/wiki/Franz_Brentano "Franz Brentano"). From here he went on to study theology and [scholastic philosophy](/wiki/Scholastic_philosophy "Scholastic philosophy") at the [University of Freiburg](/wiki/Albert-Ludwigs-Universit%C3%A4t "Albert-Ludwigs-Universität").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2019chapter_1-6)

In 1911, he broke off training for the priesthood and turned his attention to recent philosophy, in particular, [Edmund Husserl](/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl")'s *[Logical Investigations](/wiki/Logical_Investigations_(Husserl) "Logical Investigations (Husserl)")*. He graduated with a thesis on [psychologism](/wiki/Psychologism "Psychologism"), *The Doctrine of Judgment in Psychologism: A Critical-theoretical Contribution to Logic*, in 1914. The following year, he completed his [habilitation thesis](/wiki/Habilitation_thesis "Habilitation thesis") on [Duns Scotus](/wiki/Duns_Scotus "Duns Scotus"), which was directed by [Heinrich Rickert](/wiki/Heinrich_Rickert "Heinrich Rickert"), a [neo-Kantian](/wiki/Neo-Kantian "Neo-Kantian"), and influenced by Husserl's [phenomenology](/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlfieri20156-7)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2019chapter_1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuft2015461-8) The title has been published in several languages and in English is *Duns Scotus's Doctrine of Categories and Meaning*.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

He attempted to get the (Catholic) philosophy post at the University of Freiburg on 23 June 1916 but failed despite the support of [Heinrich Finke](/w/index.php?title=Heinrich_Finke&action=edit&redlink=1 "Heinrich Finke (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heinrich_Finke "de:Heinrich Finke")].[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAltman201279-10) Instead, he worked first as an unsalaried [Privatdozent](/wiki/Privatdozent "Privatdozent") then served as a soldier during the final year of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). His service was in the last ten months of the war, most of which he spent in meteorological unit on the [western front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)") upon being deemed unfit for combat.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2019chapter_1-6)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKorab-Karpowicz§1-11)

From 1919 to 1923, Heidegger taught courses at the [University of Freiburg](/wiki/University_of_Freiburg "University of Freiburg").[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[b]](#cite_note-13) At this time he also became an assistant to Edmund Husserl, who had been a professor there since 1916.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2019chapter_1-6)

### Marburg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Marburg")]

In 1923, Heidegger was elected to an [extraordinary professorship](/wiki/Extraordinary_professor "Extraordinary professor") in philosophy at the [University of Marburg](/wiki/University_of_Marburg "University of Marburg"). His colleagues there included [Rudolf Bultmann](/wiki/Rudolf_Bultmann "Rudolf Bultmann"), [Nicolai Hartmann](/wiki/Nicolai_Hartmann "Nicolai Hartmann"), [Paul Tillich](/wiki/Paul_Tillich "Paul Tillich"), and [Paul Natorp](/wiki/Paul_Natorp "Paul Natorp").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrossKemmann200565-14) Heidegger's students at Marburg included [Hans-Georg Gadamer](/wiki/Hans-Georg_Gadamer "Hans-Georg Gadamer"), [Helene Weiss](/wiki/Helene_Weiss "Helene Weiss"), [Karl Löwith](/wiki/Karl_L%C3%B6with "Karl Löwith"), [Hannah Arendt](/wiki/Hannah_Arendt "Hannah Arendt"), [Gerhard Krüger](/wiki/Gerhard_Kr%C3%BCger_(philosopher) "Gerhard Krüger (philosopher)"), [Leo Strauss](/wiki/Leo_Strauss "Leo Strauss"), [Jacob Klein](/wiki/Jacob_Klein_(philosopher) "Jacob Klein (philosopher)"), [Günther Anders](/wiki/G%C3%BCnther_Anders "Günther Anders"), and [Hans Jonas](/wiki/Hans_Jonas "Hans Jonas"). Following [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle"), he began to develop in his lectures the main theme of his philosophy: the question of the sense of being. He extended the concept of subject to the dimension of history and concrete [existence](/wiki/Existence "Existence"), which he found prefigured in such Christian thinkers as [Paul of Tarsus](/wiki/Paul_of_Tarsus "Paul of Tarsus"), [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo"), [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther"), and [Søren Kierkegaard](/wiki/S%C3%B8ren_Kierkegaard "Søren Kierkegaard"). He also read the works of [Wilhelm Dilthey](/wiki/Wilhelm_Dilthey "Wilhelm Dilthey"), Husserl, [Max Scheler](/wiki/Max_Scheler "Max Scheler"), and [Friedrich Nietzsche](/wiki/Friedrich_Nietzsche "Friedrich Nietzsche").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§1-15)

In 1927, Heidegger published his main work, *[Sein und Zeit](/wiki/Being_and_Time "Being and Time")* (*Being and Time*). He was primarily concerned in qualifying to be a full professor.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2019chapter_1-6) The book, however, did more than this: it raised him to "a position of international intellectual visibility."[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§1-15)

### Freiburg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Freiburg")]

When Husserl retired as professor of philosophy in 1928, Heidegger accepted Freiburg's election to be his successor, in spite of a counteroffer by Marburg. The title of his 1929 inaugural lecture was "What is Metaphysics?" In this year he also published *[Kant and the Problem of Metaphysics](/wiki/Kant_and_the_Problem_of_Metaphysics "Kant and the Problem of Metaphysics")*.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2019chapter_1-6)

Heidegger remained at [Freiburg im Breisgau](/wiki/Freiburg_im_Breisgau "Freiburg im Breisgau") for the rest of his life, declining later offers, including one from [Humboldt University of Berlin](/wiki/Humboldt_University_of_Berlin "Humboldt University of Berlin"). His students at Freiburg included Hannah Arendt, [Günther Anders](/wiki/G%C3%BCnther_Anders "Günther Anders"), [Hans Jonas](/wiki/Hans_Jonas "Hans Jonas"), [Karl Löwith](/wiki/Karl_L%C3%B6with "Karl Löwith"), [Charles Malik](/wiki/Charles_Malik "Charles Malik"), [Herbert Marcuse](/wiki/Herbert_Marcuse "Herbert Marcuse"), and [Ernst Nolte](/wiki/Ernst_Nolte "Ernst Nolte").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolin2015-16)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleischacker2008-17) [Emmanuel Levinas](/wiki/Emmanuel_Levinas "Emmanuel Levinas") attended his lecture courses during his stay in Freiburg in 1928, as did [Jan Patočka](/wiki/Jan_Pato%C4%8Dka "Jan Patočka") in 1933; Patočka, in particular, was deeply influenced by him.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinfels1995-18)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFindlay200232-19)

Heidegger was elected rector of the university on 21 April 1933; he joined the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") on 1 May, just three months after [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") was appointed chancellor.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBambach200382-20)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2019chapter_1-6) During his time as rector he was a member and an enthusiastic supporter of the party.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHemming2013[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidfjC-hf2kFsICnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA140redir_escyvonepageqffalse_chapter_7]-21)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarin2016-22) There is continuing controversy as to [the relationship between his philosophy and his political allegiance to Nazism](/wiki/Martin_Heidegger_and_Nazism "Martin Heidegger and Nazism").[[21]](#cite_note-:1-23)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheehan1988-24) He wanted to position himself as the philosopher of the party, but the highly abstract nature of his work and the opposition of [Alfred Rosenberg](/wiki/Alfred_Rosenberg "Alfred Rosenberg"), who himself aspired to act in that position, limited Heidegger's role. His withdrawal from his position as rector owed more to his frustration as an administrator than to any principled opposition to the Nazis, according to historians.[[23]](#cite_note-:2-25) In his inaugural address as rector on 27 May he expressed his support of a German revolution, and in an article and a speech to the students from the same year he also vocalized support for Adolf Hitler.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoung19983,_11-26) In November 1933, Heidegger signed the *[Vow of allegiance of the Professors of the German Universities and High-Schools to Adolf Hitler and the National Socialistic State](/wiki/Vow_of_allegiance_of_the_Professors_of_the_German_Universities_and_High-Schools_to_Adolf_Hitler_and_the_National_Socialistic_State "Vow of allegiance of the Professors of the German Universities and High-Schools to Adolf Hitler and the National Socialistic State")*. Heidegger resigned from the rectorate in April 1934 and pursued a teaching agenda aligned with Nazi cultural politics until 1945.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

In 1935, he gave the talk "[The Origin of the Work of Art](/wiki/The_Origin_of_the_Work_of_Art "The Origin of the Work of Art")". The next year, while in Rome, Heidegger gave his first lecture on [Friedrich Hölderlin](/wiki/Friedrich_H%C3%B6lderlin "Friedrich Hölderlin"). In the years 1936–1937, Heidegger wrote what some commentators consider his second greatest work, *[Contributions to Philosophy](/wiki/Contributions_to_Philosophy "Contributions to Philosophy")*; it would not be published, however, until 1989, 13 years after his death.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§1-15)

From 1936 to 1940, Heidegger also delivered a series of lectures on [Friedrich Nietzsche](/wiki/Friedrich_Nietzsche "Friedrich Nietzsche") at Freiburg that presented much of the raw material incorporated in his more established work and thought from this time. These would appear in published form in 1961. This period also marks the beginning of his interest in the "[essence of technology"](/wiki/The_Question_Concerning_Technology "The Question Concerning Technology").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2019chapter_1-6)

In the autumn of 1944, Heidegger was drafted into the *[Volkssturm](/wiki/Volkssturm "Volkssturm")* and assigned to dig anti-tank ditches along the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2014-28)

### Post-war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Post-war")]

In late 1946, as France engaged in *[épuration légale](/wiki/%C3%89puration_l%C3%A9gale "Épuration légale")* in its [occupation zone](/wiki/Occupation_of_Germany "Occupation of Germany"), the French military authorities determined that Heidegger should be blocked from teaching or participating in any university activities because of his association with the Nazi Party.[[c]](#cite_note-29) Nevertheless, he presented the talk "What are Poets for?" in memory of [Rilke](/wiki/Rilke "Rilke"). He also published "On Humanism" in 1947 to clarify his differences with [Jean-Paul Sartre](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sartre "Jean-Paul Sartre") and French [existentialism](/wiki/Existentialism "Existentialism"). The [denazification](/wiki/Denazification "Denazification") procedures against Heidegger continued until March 1949 when he was finally pronounced a *[Mitläufer](/wiki/Mitl%C3%A4ufer "Mitläufer")* (the second lowest of five categories of "incrimination" by association with the Nazi regime). No punitive measures against him were proposed.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESafranski1998-30) This opened the way for his readmission to teaching at Freiburg University in the winter semester of 1950–51.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESafranski1998373-31) He was granted [emeritus](/wiki/Emeritus "Emeritus") status and then taught regularly from 1951 until 1958, and by invitation until 1967.

In 1966, he gave an interview to *[Der Spiegel](/wiki/Der_Spiegel "Der Spiegel")* attempting to justify his support of the Nazi Party. Per their agreement, it was not published until five days after his death in 1976, under the title "[Only a God Can Save Us](/wiki/Only_a_God_Can_Save_Us "Only a God Can Save Us")" after a reference to Hölderlin that Heidegger makes during the interview.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2019chapter_1-6)

Heidegger's publications during this time were mostly reworked versions of his lectures. In his final days, he also arranged for a complete edition of his works, [Heidegger Gesamtausgabe](/wiki/Heidegger_Gesamtausgabe "Heidegger Gesamtausgabe"), to be compiled and published. The first volume appeared in 1975. The collection contains 106 volumes and is complete as of October 2025.[[29]](#cite_note-32)[[30]](#cite_note-33)

Martin Heidegger died in his sleep on the morning of 26 May 1976 at the age of 86 at his home in Freiburg. He was buried two days later in his birthplace of [Meßkirch](/wiki/Me%C3%9Fkirch "Meßkirch"), [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany"), beside his parents in the village graveyard he had walked past each day on his way to school.[[31]](#cite_note-:3-34) His gravestone is inscribed with a single star, which refers to a sentence he wrote in 1947: "To think is to confine yourself to a single thought that one day stands still like a star in the world’s sky."[[31]](#cite_note-:3-34)

## Early influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Early influences")]

[Edmund Husserl](/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl"), the founder of [phenomenology](/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)"), was Heidegger's teacher and a major influence on his thought. While the specific lines of influence remain a matter of scholarly dispute,[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGadamer1994[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidASDzLqTaCXsClpgPP1hlcspgPA18redir_escyvonepageqffalse_18]-35)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDostal1993142-36)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKisielBuren1994244-37) one thing is clear: Heidegger's early work on *Being and Time* moved away from Husserl's theory of [intentionality](/wiki/Intentionality "Intentionality") to focus on the pre-theoretical conditions that enable consciousness to grasp objects.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§2.1-38)

[Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") influenced Heidegger from an early age. This influence was mediated through Catholic [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), [medieval philosophy](/wiki/Medieval_philosophy "Medieval philosophy"), and [Franz Brentano](/wiki/Franz_Brentano "Franz Brentano").[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrell1975-39)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoran2000-40)
According to scholar [Michael Wheeler](/wiki/Michael_Wheeler_(philosopher) "Michael Wheeler (philosopher)"), it is by way of a "radical rethinking" of Aristotle's *[Metaphysics](/wiki/Metaphysics_(Aristotle) "Metaphysics (Aristotle)")* that Heidegger supplants Husserl's notion of intentionality with his unitary notion of being-in-the-world. According to this reinterpretation, the various modes of being are united in more basic capacity of taking-as or making-present-to.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§2.1-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Wilhelm_Dilthey_zZ_seiner_Verlobung.jpg/250px-Wilhelm_Dilthey_zZ_seiner_Verlobung.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilhelm_Dilthey_zZ_seiner_Verlobung.jpg)

Dilthey, c. 1855

The works of [Wilhelm Dilthey](/wiki/Wilhelm_Dilthey "Wilhelm Dilthey") shaped Heidegger's very early project of developing a "hermeneutics of [factical](/wiki/Facticity "Facticity") life", and his hermeneutical transformation of phenomenology.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERockmore2003477–494-41)[[d]](#cite_note-43) There is little doubt that Heidegger seized upon Dilthey's concept of hermeneutics.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrmistonSchrift199032–33-44) Heidegger's novel ideas about ontology required a *gestalt* formation, not merely a series of logical arguments, in order to demonstrate his fundamentally new paradigm of thinking, and the [hermeneutic circle](/wiki/Hermeneutic_circle "Hermeneutic circle") offered a new and powerful tool for the articulation and realization of these ideas.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson2014109–28-45)

[Søren Kierkegaard](/wiki/S%C3%B8ren_Kierkegaard "Søren Kierkegaard") contributed much to Heidegger's treatment of the existentialist aspects of his thought located in Division II of *Being and Time*.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus1991Appendix-46) Heidegger's concepts of anxiety (*[Angst](/wiki/Angst "Angst")*) and mortality draw on Kierkegaard and are indebted to the way in which the latter lays out the importance of our subjective relation to truth, our existence in the face of death, the temporality of existence, and the importance of passionate affirmation of one's individual being-in-the-world.

## Philosophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Philosophy")]

See also: [Heideggerian terminology](/wiki/Heideggerian_terminology "Heideggerian terminology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Heideggerrundweg0013.JPG/250px-Heideggerrundweg0013.JPG)](/wiki/File:Heideggerrundweg0013.JPG)

View from Heidegger's vacation chalet in Todtnauberg. Heidegger wrote most of *Being and Time* there.

### Fundamental ontology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Fundamental ontology")]

See also: [Fundamental ontology](/wiki/Fundamental_ontology "Fundamental ontology")

According to scholar [Taylor Carman](/wiki/Taylor_Carman "Taylor Carman"), traditional ontology asks "Why is there anything?", whereas Heidegger's [fundamental ontology](/wiki/Fundamental_ontology "Fundamental ontology") asks "What does it mean for something to be?" Heidegger's ontology "is fundamental relative to traditional ontology in that it concerns what any understanding of entities necessarily presupposes, namely, our understanding of that by virtue of which entities are entities".[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarman20038–52-47)

This line of inquiry is "central to Heidegger's philosophy". He accuses the Western philosophical tradition of mistakenly trying to understand *being as such* as if it were an ultimate entity.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDahlstrom2004-48) Heidegger modifies traditional ontology by focusing instead on the *meaning of being*. This kind of ontological inquiry, he claims, is required to understand the basis of our understanding, scientific and otherwise.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger1962§3-49)

In short, before asking what exists, Heidegger contends that people must first examine what "to exist" even means.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§2.2.1-50)

### *Being and Time*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Being and Time")]

See also: [Being and Time](/wiki/Being_and_Time "Being and Time")

In his first major work, *Being and Time*, Heidegger pursues this ontological inquiry by way of an analysis of the kind of being that people have, namely, that humans are the sort of beings able to pose the question of the meaning of being. According to Canadian philosopher [Sean McGrath](/wiki/Sean_McGrath_(philosopher) "Sean McGrath (philosopher)"), Heidegger was probably influenced by Scotus in this approach.[[47]](#cite_note-51) His term for us, in this phenomenological context, is [Dasein](/wiki/Dasein "Dasein").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandkühler2010-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/33/Being_and_Time_%28German_edition%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Being_and_Time_(German_edition).jpg)

Title page of first edition of *Being and Time*

This procedure works because Dasein's *pre-ontological* understanding of being shapes experience. Dasein's ordinary and even mundane experience of "being-in-the-world" provides "access to the meaning" or "sense of being"; that is, the terms in which "something becomes intelligible as something."[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger1962151-53) Heidegger proposes that this ordinary "prescientific" understanding precedes abstract ways of knowing, such as logic or theory.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger196212-54) *Being and Time* is designed to show how this implicit understanding can be made progressively explicit through [phenomenology](/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)") and [hermeneutics](/wiki/Hermeneutics#Heidegger_(1889–1976) "Hermeneutics").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger19621–27-55)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhittingham2018-56)[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood1999-57)

#### Being-in-the-world

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Being-in-the-world")]

Heidegger introduces the term [Dasein](/wiki/Dasein "Dasein") to denote a "living being" through its activity of "being there".[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorrigan-KellyMillarDowling2016-58) Understood as a unitary phenomenon rather than a contingent, additive combination, it is characterized by Heidegger as "being-in-the-world".[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§2.2.3-59)

Heidegger insists that the 'in' of Dasein's being-in-the-world is an 'in' of involvement or of engagement, not of objective, physical enclosedness. The sense in which Dasein is 'in' the world is the sense of "residing" or "dwelling" in the world.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger196254-60) Heidegger provides a few examples: "having to do with something, producing something, attending to something and looking after it, making use of something".[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger196256-61)[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolt1999[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidpaxTAQAAQBAJnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA46hlenredir_escyvonepageqffalse_46–49]-62)

Just as 'being-in' does not denote objective, physical enclosedness, so 'world', as Heidegger uses the term, does not denote a universe of physical objects. The world, in Heidegger's sense, is to be understood according to our sense of our possibilities: things present themselves to people in terms of their projects, the uses to which they can put them. The 'sight' with which people grasp equipment is not a mentalistic intentionality, but what Heidegger calls 'circumspection'.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger196269-63) This is to say that equipment reveals itself in terms of its 'towards-which,' in terms of the work it is good for. In the everyday world, people are absorbed within the equipmental totality of their work-world.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolt1999pp._[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidpaxTAQAAQBAJnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA46hlenredir_escyvonepageqffalse_46–61],_esp._diagram_on_p._61-64)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus199188–100-65) Moreover, on Heidegger's analysis, this entails a radical holism.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§2.2.3-59) In his own words, "there 'is' no such thing as *an* equipment".[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger1962p._97,_qtd._in_translators'_fn._1-66)

For example, when someone sits down to dinner and picks up their fork, they are not picking up an object with good stabbing properties: they are non-reflectively engaging an 'in-order-to-eat'. When it works as expected, equipment is transparent; when it is used, it is subsumed under the work toward which it is employed. Heidegger calls this structure of practically ordered reference relations the 'worldhood of the world'.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolt1999pp._[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidpaxTAQAAQBAJnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA46hlenredir_escyvonepageqffalse_46–61],_esp._diagram_on_p._61-64)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus199188–100-65)

Heidegger calls the mode of being of such entities "ready-to-hand", for they are understood only in being handled.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolt1999[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidpaxTAQAAQBAJnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA50hlenredir_escyvonepageqffalse_50]-67) If the fork is made of plastic, however, and it snaps in the course of using it, then it assumes the mode of being that Heidegger calls "present-at-hand." For now the fork needs to be made the object of focal awareness, considering it in terms of its properties. Is it too broken to use? If so, could the diner possibly get by with another utensil or just with their fingers? This kind of equipmental breakdown is not the only way that objects become present-at-hand for us, but Heidegger considers it typical of the way that this shift occurs in the course of ordinary goings-on.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus199171–72-68)

In this way, Heidegger creates a theoretical space for the categories of subject and object, while at the same time denying that they apply to our most basic way of moving about in the world, of which they are instead presented as derivative.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§2.2.2-69)

Heidegger presents three primary structural features of being-in-the-world: understanding, attunement, and discourse. He calls these features "existentiales" or "existentialia" (*Existenzialien*) to distinguish their ontological status, as distinct from the "categories" of metaphysics.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus199140–42-70)[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood199961–62-71)

* *Understanding* is "our fundamental ability to be someone, to do things, to get around in the world". It is the basic "know-how" in terms of which Dasein goes about pursuing the usually humdrum tasks that make up daily life. Heidegger argues that this mode of understanding is more fundamental than theoretical understanding.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolt1999[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidpaxTAQAAQBAJnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA65hlenredir_escyvonepageqffalse_65]-72)
* *Attunement* is "our way of finding ourselves thrust into the world".[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolt1999[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidpaxTAQAAQBAJnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA65hlenredir_escyvonepageqffalse_65]-72) It can also be translated as "disposition" or "affectedness". (The standard translation of Macquarrie and Robinson uses "state-of-mind", but this misleadingly suggests a private mental state.) There is no perfect equivalent for Heidegger's *Befindlichkeit*, which is not even an ordinary German word.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger1996axv-73)[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus1991168-74)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolt1999[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidpaxTAQAAQBAJnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA65hlenredir_escyvonepageqffalse_65]-72) What needs to be conveyed, however, is "being found in a situation where things and opinions already matter".[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus1991168-74)
* *Discourse* (sometimes: *talk* or *telling* [*Rede*]) is "the articulation of the world into recognizable, communicable patterns of meaning." It is implicated in both understanding and attunement: "The world that is opened up by moods and grasped by understanding gets organized by discourse. Discourse makes language possible."[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolt1999[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidpaxTAQAAQBAJnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA65hlenredir_escyvonepageqffalse_65]-72) According to Heidegger, "Discourse is the articulation of intelligibility."[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger1962161-75) In its most basic form, this referential whole manifests itself in the way things are told apart just in the course of using them.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus1991214-76)

Heidegger unifies these three existential features of Dasein in a composite structure he terms "care":[[e]](#cite_note-78) "ahead-of-itself-being-already-in-(the-world) as being-amidst (entities encountered within-the-world)."[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger1962192-79)[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus1991238–39-80) What unifies this formula is *temporality*. Understanding is oriented towards future possibilities, attunement is shaped by the past, and discourse discloses the present in those terms.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus1991244–45-81)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§2.2.7-82) In this way, the investigation into the being of Dasein leads to time. Much of Division II of *Being and Time* is devoted to a more fundamental reinterpretation of the findings of Division I in terms of Dasein's temporality.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolt1999[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidpaxTAQAAQBAJlpgPP1hlenpgPA85_85ff]-83)

#### *Das Man*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Das Man")]

As implied in the analysis of both attunement and discourse, Dasein is "always already", or [a priori](/wiki/A_priori "A priori"), a social being. In Heidegger's technical idiom, Dasein is "Dasein-with" (*Mitsein*), which he presents as equally primordial with "being-one's self" (*Selbstsein*).[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood199931-84)

Heidegger's term for this existential feature of Dasein is *das Man*, which is a German pronoun, *man*, that Heidegger turns into a noun.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood1999212-85) In English it is usually translated as either "the they" or "the one" (sometimes also capitalized); for, as Heidegger puts it, "By 'others' we do not mean everyone else but me.... They are rather those from whom for the most part, one does *not* distinguish oneself—those among whom one is too".[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger1962118-86) Quite frequently the term is just left in the German.

According to philosopher [Hubert Dreyfus](/wiki/Hubert_Dreyfus "Hubert Dreyfus"), part of Heidegger's aim is to show that, contrary to Husserl, individuals do not generate an intersubjective world from their separate activities; rather, "these activities *presuppose* the disclosure of one shared world." This is one way in which Heidegger breaks from the [Cartesian](/wiki/Cartesianism "Cartesianism") tradition of beginning from the perspective of individual subjectivity.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus1991142-87)

Dreyfus argues that the chapter on *das Man* is "the most confused" in *Being and Time* and so is often misinterpreted. The problem, he says, is that Heidegger's presentation conflates two opposing influences. The first is Dilthey's account of the role that public and historical contexts have in the production of significance. The second is Kierkegaard's insistence that truth is never to be found in the crowd.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus1991143-88)

The Diltheyian dimension of Heidegger's analysis positions *das Man* as ontologically existential in the same way as understanding, affectedness, and discourse. This dimension of Heidegger's analysis captures the way that a socio-historical "background" makes possible the specific significance that entities and activities can have.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDreyfus1991143-88) Philosopher [Charles Taylor](/wiki/Charles_Taylor_(philosopher) "Charles Taylor (philosopher)") expands upon the term: "It is that of which I am not simply unaware... but at the same time I cannot be said to be explicitly or focally aware of it, because that status is already occupied by what it is making intelligible".[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1993325-89) For this reason, background non-representationally informs and enables engaged agency in the world, but is something that people can never make fully explicit to themselves.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1993327-90)

The Kierkegaardian influence on Heidegger's analysis introduces a more [existentialist](/wiki/Existentialist "Existentialist") dimension to *Being and Time*. ([Existentialism](/wiki/Existentialism "Existentialism") is a broad philosophical movement largely defined by [Jean-Paul Sartre](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sartre "Jean-Paul Sartre") and is not to be confused with Heidegger's technical analysis of the specific existential features of Dasein.) Its central notion is *authenticity*, which emerges as a problem from the "publicness" built into the existential role of *das Man*. In Heidegger's own words:

> In this inconspicuousness and unascertainability, the real dictatorship of the 'they' is unfolded. We take pleasure and enjoy ourselves as *they* take pleasure; we read, see and judge about literature and art as *they* see and judge; likewise we shrink back from the 'great mass' as *they* shrink back; we find 'shocking' what they find shocking. The 'they', which is nothing definite, and which we all are, through not as the sum, prescribes the kind of being of everydayness.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger1962164,_H._126-7-91)

This "dictatorship of *das Man*" threatens to undermine Heidegger's entire project of uncovering the meaning of being because it does not seem possible, from such a condition, to even raise the question of being that Heidegger claims to pursue. He responds to this challenge with his account of [authenticity](/wiki/Authenticity_(philosophy) "Authenticity (philosophy)").

#### Authenticity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Authenticity")]

Heidegger's term *Eigentlichkeit* is a neologism, in which Heidegger stresses the root *eigen*, meaning "own." So this word, usually translated "authenticity", could just as well be translated "ownedness" or "being one's own".[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVargaGuignon2014§3.1-92)[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood199922–23-93) Authenticity, according to Heidegger, is a matter of taking responsibility for being, that is, the stand that people take with respect to their ultimate projects. It is, in his terms, a matter of taking a properly "resolute" stand on "for-the-sake-of-which". Put differently, the "self" to which one is true in authenticity is not something just "there" to be discovered, but instead is a matter of "on-going narrative construction".[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVargaGuignon2014§3.1-92)

Scholars Somogy Varga and [Charles Guignon](/wiki/Charles_Guignon "Charles Guignon") describe three ways by which Dasein might attain an authentic relation to itself from out of its "fallen" condition as "they"-self. First, a powerful mood such as [anxiety](/wiki/Angst "Angst") can disclose Dasein to itself as an ultimately isolated individual. Second, direct confrontation with Dasein's "ownmost" potential for death can similarly disclose to Dasein its own irreducible finitude. Third, experiencing "the call of conscience" can disclose to Dasein its own "guilt" (*Schuld*) as the debt it has to itself in virtue of having taken over pre-given possibilities that it is now Dasein's own responsibility to maintain.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVargaGuignon2014§3.1-92)

Philosopher [Michael E. Zimmerman](/wiki/Michael_E._Zimmerman "Michael E. Zimmerman") describes authenticity as "resolving to accept the openness which, paradoxically, one already is".[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmerman1981xx-94) He emphasizes that this is a matter, not of "intellectual comprehension", but of "hard-won insight".[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmerman1981xix-95) Authenticity is ultimately a matter of allowing the ego to be "eclipsed by the manifestation of one's finitude".[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmerman1981xxvii-96)

Although the term "authenticity" disappears from Heidegger's writing after *Being and Time*, Zimmerman argues that it is supplanted in his later thought by the less subjective or [voluntaristic](/wiki/Voluntarism_(philosophy) "Voluntarism (philosophy)") notion of *Ereignis*. This ordinary German term for "event" or "happening" is theorized by Heidegger as the appropriation of Dasein into a cosmic play of concealment and appearance.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmerman1981xxiv–xxviii-97)

### Later works: The Turn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Later works: The Turn")]

See also: [Kehre](/wiki/Kehre "Kehre")

Heidegger's "Turn", which is sometimes referred to by the German *die [Kehre](/wiki/Kehre "Kehre")*, refers to a change in his work as early as 1930 that became clearly established by the 1940s, according to some commentators, who variously describe a shift of focus or a major change in outlook.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKorab-Karpowicz§1-11)[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichardson1963-98)[[f]](#cite_note-100)

Heidegger himself frequently used the term to refer to the shift announced at the end of *Being and Time* from "being and time" to "time and being". However, he rejected the existence of the "sharp 'about turn'" posited by some interpreters.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood19998-101) Scholar [Michael Inwood](/wiki/Michael_Inwood "Michael Inwood") also calls attention to the fact that many of the ideas from *Being and Time* are retained in a different vocabulary in his later work—and also that, in other cases, a word or expression common throughout his career comes to acquire a different meaning in the later works.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood19998-101)

This supposed shift—applied here to cover about 30 years of Heidegger's 40-year writing career—has been described by commentators from widely varied viewpoints, for instance, from *dwelling* (being) in the world to *doing* (temporality) in the world.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§3.1-99)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger2002-102) This aspect, in particular the 1951 essay "Building Dwelling Thinking", has influenced several architectural theorists.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies2017-103)

Other interpreters believe the Turn can be overstated or does not exist at all. For instance, [Thomas Sheehan](/wiki/Thomas_Sheehan_(philosopher) "Thomas Sheehan (philosopher)") believes this supposed change is "far less dramatic than usually suggested", and entails merely a change in focus and method.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoltFried200115-104) [Mark Wrathall](/wiki/Mark_Wrathall "Mark Wrathall") argued that the Turn is not found in Heidegger's writings, but is simply a misconception.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWrathall2010-105)

Some notable later works are "[The Origin of the Work of Art](/wiki/The_Origin_of_the_Work_of_Art "The Origin of the Work of Art")" (1935), *[Contributions to Philosophy](/wiki/Contributions_to_Philosophy "Contributions to Philosophy")* (1937), "[Letter on Humanism](/wiki/Letter_on_Humanism "Letter on Humanism")" (1946), "Building Dwelling Thinking" (1951), "[The Question Concerning Technology](/wiki/The_Question_Concerning_Technology "The Question Concerning Technology")" (1954), and "[What Is Called Thinking?](/wiki/What_Is_Called_Thinking%3F "What Is Called Thinking?")" (1954).

### The history of being

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: The history of being")]

The idea of asking about being may be traced back via Aristotle to [Parmenides](/wiki/Parmenides "Parmenides"). Heidegger claims to revive this question of being that had been largely forgotten by the [metaphysical](/wiki/Metaphysics "Metaphysics") tradition extending from [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") to [Descartes](/wiki/Descartes "Descartes"), a forgetfulness extending into the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), as well as modern science and technology. In pursuit of the retrieval of the question, Heidegger spends considerable time reflecting on [ancient Greek thought](/wiki/Greek_philosophy "Greek philosophy"), in particular on Plato, [Parmenides](/wiki/Parmenides "Parmenides"), [Heraclitus](/wiki/Heraclitus "Heraclitus"), and [Anaximander](/wiki/Anaximander "Anaximander").

In his later philosophy, Heidegger attempts to reconstruct the "history of being" in order to show how the different epochs in the history of philosophy were dominated by different conceptions of being.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood1999§History_of_being-106) His goal is to retrieve the original experience of being present in the [early Greek thought](/wiki/Pre-Socratic_philosophy "Pre-Socratic philosophy") that was covered up by later philosophers.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKorab-Karpowicz§4-107)

According to [W. Julian Korab-Karpowicz](/wiki/Wlodzimierz_Julian_Korab-Karpowicz "Wlodzimierz Julian Korab-Karpowicz"), Heidegger believed "the thinking of [Heraclitus](/wiki/Heraclitus "Heraclitus") and [Parmenides](/wiki/Parmenides "Parmenides"), which lies at the origin of philosophy, was falsified and misinterpreted" by Plato and Aristotle, thus tainting all of subsequent Western philosophy.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKorab-Karpowicz201658-108) In his *[Introduction to Metaphysics](/wiki/Introduction_to_Metaphysics_(Heidegger_book) "Introduction to Metaphysics (Heidegger book)")*, Heidegger states, "Among the most ancient Greek thinkers, it is Heraclitus who was subjected to the most fundamentally un-Greek misinterpretation in the course of Western history, and who nevertheless in more recent times has provided the strongest impulses toward redisclosing what is authentically Greek."[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger2014-109)

[Charles Guignon](/wiki/Charles_Guignon "Charles Guignon") writes that Heidegger aims to correct this misunderstanding by reviving Presocratic notions of being with an emphasis on "understanding the way beings show up in (and as) an unfolding *happening or event*." Guignon adds that "we might call this alternative outlook 'event ontology.'"[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoltFried200136-110)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Language")]

In *Being and Time*, language is presented as logically secondary to Dasein's understanding of the world and its significance. On this conception of worldhood, language can develop from prelinguistic significance.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood1999208-111)

Post-turn, Heidegger refines his position to present some basic words (e.g., *phusis*, the Greek term that roughly translates to "nature") as world-disclosive, that is, as establishing the foundational parameters in terms of which Dasein's understanding can occur in the specific ways that it does. It is in this context that Heidegger proclaims that "Language is the house of being."[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood1999209-112)

In the present age, he says, the language of "technology", or instrumental reason, flatten the significance of our world. For salvation, he turns to poetry.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood1999209-112)

Heidegger rejected the notion of language being purely a means of communication. Language construed as such, he believed, would form the basis of an age of technology, the digital thought processes of which would only use language to organise and communicate the coverage of that which exists. Thinking in terms of calculation and digital processing would put man at odds with language, at the centre of everything that exists. If man would believe that they would have language at their disposal, that they would be the one to use it, then, Heidegger believed, man would completely miss the core tenet of language itself:[[107]](#cite_note-113) "It is language that speaks, not man. Man only speaks if they neatly correspond to language."[[108]](#cite_note-114) In this way, Heidegger wanted to point out that man is only a *participant* of language that they have not themselves created. Man is bound within a sort of process of transfer and may only *act* with respect to anything the language conveys.

In this, however, Heidegger does not think in terms of [philosophy of culture](/wiki/Philosophy_of_culture "Philosophy of culture"): The [tautology](/wiki/Tautology_(language) "Tautology (language)") of the formulation "**language speaks**" (originally in German "die Sprache spricht") is his way of trying to prevent the phenomenon of language to be used with respect to anything else than language itself. In line with his unique thinking, he is seeking to avoid having to justify the language by anything else. In this way, language could for instance never be explained by the sheer transmission of acoustic sounds, or speaking. According to Heidegger, language is rather difficult to fathom because we are too close to it, hence we need to speak about that which usually remains unmentioned because it is just too close to us. His work "Unterwegs zur Sprache" (*On the way to language*) is an attempt to reach "a place we already are in."[[109]](#cite_note-115)

### Influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Influences")]

[![Friedrich Nietzsche](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Nietzsche187a.jpg/250px-Nietzsche187a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nietzsche187a.jpg)

[![Friedrich Hölderlin,](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/FK_Hiemer_-_Friedrich_H%C3%B6lderlin_%28Pastell_1792%29.jpg/250px-FK_Hiemer_-_Friedrich_H%C3%B6lderlin_%28Pastell_1792%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FK_Hiemer_-_Friedrich_H%C3%B6lderlin_(Pastell_1792).jpg)

Heidegger dedicated many of his lectures to both Nietzsche and Hölderlin.

[Friedrich Nietzsche](/wiki/Friedrich_Nietzsche "Friedrich Nietzsche") and [Friedrich Hölderlin](/wiki/Friedrich_H%C3%B6lderlin "Friedrich Hölderlin") were both important influences on Heidegger, and many of his lecture courses were devoted to one or the other, especially in the 1930s and 1940s.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaffoulNelson2013[httpsbooksgooglecombooksideSZMAQAAQBAJpgPT224redir_escyvonepageqffalse_224]-116) The lectures on Nietzsche focused on fragments posthumously published under the title *[The Will to Power](/wiki/The_Will_to_Power_(manuscript) "The Will to Power (manuscript)")*, rather than on Nietzsche's published works. Heidegger reads *The Will to Power* as the culminating expression of Western metaphysics, and the lectures are a kind of dialogue between the two thinkers.

[Michael Allen Gillespie](/wiki/Michael_Allen_Gillespie "Michael Allen Gillespie") says that Heidegger's theoretical acceptance of "destiny" has much in common with the [millenarianism](/wiki/Millenarianism "Millenarianism") of Marxism. But Marxists believe Heidegger's "theoretical acceptance is antagonistic to practical political activity and implies fascism". Gillespie, however, says "the real danger" from Heidegger is not [quietism](/wiki/Quietism_(philosophy) "Quietism (philosophy)") but [fanaticism](/wiki/Fanaticism "Fanaticism"). Modernity has cast mankind toward a new goal "on the brink of profound [nihilism](/wiki/Nihilism "Nihilism")" that is "so alien it requires the construction of a new tradition to make it comprehensible."[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGillespie1984133-117)

Gillespie extrapolates from Heidegger's writings that humankind may degenerate into "scientists, workers, and brutes".[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGillespie1984[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidfApCCQAAQBAJpgPT148redir_escyvonepageqffalse_148]-118) According to Gillespie, Heidegger envisaged this abyss to be the greatest event in the history of the West because it might enable humanity to comprehend being more profoundly and primordially than the [Presocratics](/wiki/Presocratics "Presocratics").[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGillespie1984[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidfApCCQAAQBAJnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA151hlenredir_escyvonepageqffalse_151]-119)

The poetry of Friedrich Hölderlin became an increasingly central focus of Heidegger's later work and thought. Heidegger grants Hölderlin a singular place within the history of being and the history of Germany, as a herald whose thought is yet to be "heard" in Germany or the West more generally. Many of Heidegger's works from the 1930s onwards include meditations on lines from Hölderlin's poetry, and several of the lecture courses are devoted to the reading of a single poem; for example, *[Hölderlin's Hymn "The Ister"](/wiki/H%C3%B6lderlin%27s_Hymn_%22The_Ister%22 "Hölderlin's Hymn \"The Ister\"")*.
