### The rectorate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: The rectorate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Universit%C3%A4t_Freiburg_Kollegiengeb%C3%A4ude_I_%28Altbau%29.jpg/250px-Universit%C3%A4t_Freiburg_Kollegiengeb%C3%A4ude_I_%28Altbau%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Universit%C3%A4t_Freiburg_Kollegiengeb%C3%A4ude_I_(Altbau).jpg)

The [University of Freiburg](/wiki/University_of_Freiburg "University of Freiburg"), where Heidegger was Rector from 21 April 1933 to 23 April 1934

[Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") was sworn in as [Chancellor of Germany](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany_(German_Reich) "Chancellor of Germany (German Reich)") on 30 January 1933. Heidegger was elected [rector](/wiki/Rector_(academia) "Rector (academia)") of the [University of Freiburg](/wiki/University_of_Freiburg "University of Freiburg") on 21 April 1933, and assumed the position the following day. On 1 May, he joined the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party").

Beginning in February 1933 Heidegger was active, alongside [Ernst Krieck](/wiki/Ernst_Krieck "Ernst Krieck"), in an organization known as the Cultural-Political W*orking Group of German University Instructors (Kulturpolitische Arbeitsgemeinschaft deutscher Hochschullehrer*). The group met privately in the Spring of 1933 to plan the [Nazification](/wiki/Gleichschaltung "Gleichschaltung") of the German university system.[[23]](#cite_note-:2-25)

On 27 May 1933, Heidegger delivered his inaugural address, the *Rektoratsrede* (titled "The Self-assertion of the German University"), in a hall decorated with swastikas, with members of the [Sturmabteilung](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung") (SA) and prominent Nazi Party officials present.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharpe2018-120)

That summer he delivered a lecture on a fragment of [Heraclitus](/wiki/Heraclitus "Heraclitus") (usually translated in English: "War is the father of all"). His notes on this lecture appear under the heading "Struggle as the essence of Beings."[[115]](#cite_note-:0-121) In this lecture he suggests that if an enemy cannot be found for the people then one must be invented, and once conceptualized and identified, then the 'beings' who have discovered or invented this enemy must strive for the total annihilation of the enemy.[[115]](#cite_note-:0-121)

His tenure as rector was fraught with difficulties from the outset. Some [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi "Nazi") education officials viewed him as a rival, while others saw his efforts as comical. Some of Heidegger's fellow Nazis also ridiculed his philosophical writings as gibberish. He finally offered his resignation as rector on 23 April 1934, and it was accepted on 27 April. Heidegger remained a member of both the academic faculty and of the Nazi Party until the end of the war.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheehan1988-24)

Philosophical historian [Hans Sluga](/wiki/Hans_Sluga "Hans Sluga") wrote, "Though as rector he prevented students from displaying an [anti-Semitic](/wiki/Anti-Semitic "Anti-Semitic") poster at the entrance to the university and from holding a book burning, he kept in close contact with the Nazi student leaders and clearly signaled to them his sympathy with their activism."[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESluga2013149-122)

In 1945, Heidegger wrote of his term as rector, giving the writing to his son Hermann; it was published in 1983:

> The rectorate was an attempt to see something in the movement that had come to power, beyond all its failings and crudeness, that was much more far-reaching and that could perhaps one day bring a concentration on the Germans' Western historical essence. It will in no way be denied that at the time I believed in such possibilities and for that reason renounced the actual vocation of thinking in favor of being effective in an official capacity. In no way will what was caused by my own inadequacy in office be played down. But these points of view do not capture what is essential and what moved me to accept the rectorate.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeskeKettering199029-123)

### Treatment of Husserl

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Treatment of Husserl")]

Beginning in 1917, German-Jewish philosopher [Edmund Husserl](/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl") championed Heidegger's work, and helped Heidegger become his successor for the chair in philosophy at the University of Freiburg in 1928.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenhabib2003120-124)

On 6 April 1933, the Gauleiter of [Baden](/wiki/Baden "Baden") Province, Robert Wagner, suspended all Jewish government employees, including present and retired faculty at the University of Freiburg. Heidegger's predecessor as rector formally notified Husserl of his "enforced leave of absence" on 14 April 1933.

Heidegger became Rector of the University of Freiburg on 22 April 1933. The following week the national Reich law of 28 April 1933 replaced Reichskommissar Wagner's decree. The Reich law required the firing of Jewish professors from German universities, including those, such as Husserl, who had converted to Christianity. The termination of Husserl's academic privileges thus did not involve any specific action on Heidegger's part.

Heidegger had by then broken off contact with Husserl, other than through intermediaries. Heidegger later claimed that his relationship with Husserl had already become strained after Husserl publicly "settled accounts" with Heidegger and [Max Scheler](/wiki/Max_Scheler "Max Scheler") in the early 1930s.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger199048-125)

Heidegger did not attend his former mentor's cremation in 1938, for which he later declared himself regretful: "That I failed to express again to Husserl my gratitude and respect for him upon the occasion of his final illness and death is a human failure that I apologized for in a letter to Mrs. Husserl."[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAugsteinWolffHeidegger1976193–219-126) In 1941, under pressure from publisher [Max Niemeyer](/wiki/Max_Niemeyer_Verlag "Max Niemeyer Verlag"), Heidegger agreed to remove the dedication to Husserl from *Being and Time* (restored in post-war editions).[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESafranski1998258–58-127)

Heidegger's behavior towards Husserl has provoked controversy. Hannah Arendt initially suggested that Heidegger's behavior precipitated Husserl's death, calling Heidegger a "potential murderer". However, she later recanted her accusation.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEttinger199737-128)

### Post-rectorate period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Post-rectorate period")]

After the failure of Heidegger's rectorship, he withdrew from most political activity, but remained a member of the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party"). In May 1934 he accepted a position on the Committee for the Philosophy of Law in the [Academy for German Law](/wiki/Academy_for_German_Law "Academy for German Law"), where he remained active until at least 1936. The academy had official consultant status in preparing Nazi legislation such as the [Nuremberg racial laws](/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws "Nuremberg Laws") that came into effect in 1935. In addition to Heidegger, such Nazi notables as [Hans Frank](/wiki/Hans_Frank "Hans Frank"), [Julius Streicher](/wiki/Julius_Streicher "Julius Streicher"), [Carl Schmitt](/wiki/Carl_Schmitt "Carl Schmitt"), and [Alfred Rosenberg](/wiki/Alfred_Rosenberg "Alfred Rosenberg") belonged to the Academy and served on this committee.

In a 1935 lecture, later published in 1953 as part of the book *[Introduction to Metaphysics](/wiki/Introduction_to_Metaphysics_(Heidegger) "Introduction to Metaphysics (Heidegger)")*, Heidegger refers to the "inner truth and greatness" of the Nazi movement, but he then adds a qualifying statement in parentheses: "namely, the confrontation of planetary technology and modern humanity". However, it subsequently transpired that this qualification had not been made during the original lecture, although Heidegger claimed that it had been. This has led scholars to argue that Heidegger still supported the Nazi party in 1935 but that he did not want to admit this after the war, and the inclusion of this detail attempted to silently correct his earlier statement.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHabermas1989452–54-129)[[g]](#cite_note-130)

In private notes written in 1939, Heidegger took a strongly critical view of Hitler's ideology;[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger2016§47-131) however, in public lectures, he seems to have continued to make ambiguous comments which, if they expressed criticism of the regime, did so only in the context of praising its ideals. For instance, in a 1942 lecture, published posthumously, Heidegger said of recent German classics scholarship, "In the majority of "research results," the Greeks appear as pure National Socialists. This overenthusiasm on the part of academics seems not even to notice that with such "results" it does National Socialism and its historical uniqueness no service at all, not that it needs this anyhow."[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger1996b79–80-132)

An important witness to Heidegger's continued allegiance to Nazism during the post-rectorship period is his former student [Karl Löwith](/wiki/Karl_L%C3%B6with "Karl Löwith"), who met Heidegger in 1936 while Heidegger was visiting Rome. In an account set down in 1940 (though not intended for publication), Löwith recalled that Heidegger wore a swastika pin to their meeting, though Heidegger knew that Löwith was Jewish. Löwith also recalled that Heidegger "left no doubt about his faith in [Hitler](/wiki/Hitler "Hitler")", and stated that his support for Nazism was in agreement with the essence of his philosophy.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolin1991-133)

Heidegger rejected the "biologically grounded racism" of the Nazis, replacing it with linguistic-historical heritage.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020[httpsplatostanfordeduarchivesfall2020entriesheideggerOnlGodSavUs_§3.5]-134)

### Post-war period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Post-war period")]

After the end of World War II, Heidegger was summoned to appear at a [denazification](/wiki/Denazification "Denazification") hearing. He was charged on four counts, dismissed from the university and declared a "follower" (*[Mitläufer](/wiki/Mitl%C3%A4ufer "Mitläufer")*) of Nazism. Heidegger was forbidden to teach between 1945 and 1951. One consequence of this teaching ban was that Heidegger began to engage far more in the French philosophical scene.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJanicaud2015-135)

In his postwar thinking, Heidegger distanced himself from Nazism, but his critical comments about Nazism seem scandalous to some since they tend to equate the Nazi war atrocities with other inhumane practices related to [rationalization](/wiki/Rationalization_(sociology) "Rationalization (sociology)") and [industrialisation](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation"), including the treatment of animals by [factory farming](/wiki/Factory_farming "Factory farming"). For instance, in a lecture delivered at Bremen in 1949, Heidegger said: "Agriculture is now a motorized food industry, the same thing in its essence as the production of corpses in the gas chambers and the extermination camps, the same thing as blockades and the reduction of countries to famine, the same thing as the manufacture of hydrogen bombs."[[129]](#cite_note-136)

In 1967 Heidegger met with the Jewish poet [Paul Celan](/wiki/Paul_Celan "Paul Celan"), a concentration camp survivor. Having kept in correspondence with each other since 1956,[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELyon200666-137) Celan visited Heidegger at his country retreat and wrote an enigmatic poem about the meeting, which some interpret as Celan's wish for Heidegger to apologize for his behavior during the Nazi era.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson1991-138)

Heidegger's defenders, notably Arendt, see his support for Nazism as arguably a personal " 'error' " (a word which Arendt placed in quotation marks when referring to Heidegger's Nazi-era politics).[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurray1978293–303-139) Defenders think this error was irrelevant to Heidegger's philosophy. Critics such as Levinas, [Karl Löwith](/wiki/Karl_L%C3%B6with "Karl Löwith"),[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELöwith198957-140) and Theodor Adorno claim that Heidegger's support for Nazism revealed flaws inherent in his thought.

#### *Der Spiegel* interview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Der Spiegel interview")]

On 23 September 1966, Heidegger was interviewed by [Georg Wolff](/wiki/Georg_Wolff_(journalist) "Georg Wolff (journalist)"), a former Nazi, and [Rudolf Augstein](/wiki/Rudolf_Augstein "Rudolf Augstein") for *[Der Spiegel](/wiki/Der_Spiegel "Der Spiegel")* magazine, in which he agreed to discuss his political past provided that the interview be published posthumously. ("[Only a God Can Save Us](/wiki/Only_a_God_Can_Save_Us "Only a God Can Save Us")" was published five days after his death, on 31 May 1976.)[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAugsteinWolffHeidegger1976193–219-126) In the interview, Heidegger defended his entanglement with Nazism in two ways. First, he claimed that there was no alternative, saying that with his acceptance of the position of rector of the [University of Freiburg](/wiki/University_of_Freiburg "University of Freiburg") he was trying to save the university (and science in general) from being politicized and thus had to compromise with the Nazi administration. Second, he admitted that he saw an "awakening" (*Aufbruch*) which might help to find a "new national and social approach," but said that he changed his mind about this in 1934, when he refused, under threat of dismissal, to remove from the position of dean of the faculty those who were not acceptable to the Nazi party, and he consequently decided to resign as rector.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAugsteinWolffHeidegger1976193–219-126)

In his interview Heidegger defended as [double-speak](/wiki/Double-speak "Double-speak") his 1935 lecture describing the "inner truth and greatness of this movement." He affirmed that Nazi informants who observed his lectures would understand that by "movement" he meant Nazism. However, Heidegger asserted that his dedicated students would know this statement was not praise for the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party"). Rather, he meant it as he expressed it in the parenthetical clarification later added to *Introduction to Metaphysics* (1953), namely, "the confrontation of planetary technology and modern humanity."[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGrath200892-141)

The eyewitness account of Löwith from 1940 contradicts the account given in *Der Spiegel* interview in two ways: that he did not make any decisive break with Nazism in 1934, and that Heidegger was willing to entertain more profound relations between his philosophy and political involvement. The *Spiegel* interviewers did not bring up Heidegger's 1949 quotation comparing the industrialization of agriculture to the extermination camps. In fact, the interviewers were not in possession of much of the evidence now known for Heidegger's Nazi sympathies.[[h]](#cite_note-142) Furthermore, *Der Spiegel* journalist Georg Wolff had been an [SS-Hauptsturmführer](/wiki/Hauptsturmf%C3%BChrer "Hauptsturmführer") with the [Sicherheitsdienst](/wiki/Sicherheitsdienst "Sicherheitsdienst"), stationed in Oslo during World War II, and had been writing articles with antisemitic and racist overtones in *Der Spiegel* since the end of the war.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJanich2013178-143)

#### The Farías debate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: The Farías debate")]

[Jacques Derrida](/wiki/Jacques_Derrida "Jacques Derrida"), [Philippe Lacoue-Labarthe](/wiki/Philippe_Lacoue-Labarthe "Philippe Lacoue-Labarthe"), and [Jean-François Lyotard](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Lyotard "Jean-François Lyotard"), among others, all engaged in debate and disagreement about the relation between Heidegger's philosophy and his Nazi politics. These debates included the question of whether it was possible to do without Heidegger's philosophy, a position which Derrida in particular rejected. Forums where these debates took place include the proceedings of the first conference dedicated to Derrida's work, published as "*Les Fins de l'homme à partir du travail de Jacques Derrida: colloque de Cerisy, 23 juillet-2 août 1980*", Derrida's "*Feu la cendre/cio' che resta del fuoco*", and the studies on [Paul Celan](/wiki/Paul_Celan "Paul Celan") by Lacoue-Labarthe and Derrida, which shortly preceded the detailed studies of Heidegger's politics published in and after 1987.

#### The *Black Notebooks*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: The Black Notebooks")]

In 2014, Heidegger's *[Black Notebooks](/wiki/Black_Notebooks "Black Notebooks")* were published. Written between 1931 and the early 1970s, the notebooks contain several examples of [anti-Semitic](/wiki/Anti-Semitic "Anti-Semitic") sentiments, which led to a reevaluation of [Heidegger's relation to Nazism](/wiki/Martin_Heidegger_and_Nazism "Martin Heidegger and Nazism").[[21]](#cite_note-:1-23) An example of Heidegger using anti-Semitic language was his writing, "world [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") is ungraspable everywhere and doesn't need to get involved in military action while continuing to unfurl its influence, whereas we are left to sacrifice the best blood of the best of our people". The concept of "world Judaism" was first promoted by the anti-Semitic text *[The Protocols of the Elders of Zion](/wiki/The_Protocols_of_the_Elders_of_Zion "The Protocols of the Elders of Zion")* and later appeared in Hitler's *[Mein Kampf](/wiki/Mein_Kampf "Mein Kampf")*.[[136]](#cite_note-144) In another instance, Heidegger wrote "by living according to the principle of race [Jews] had themselves promoted the very reasoning by which they were now being attacked and so they had no right to complain when it was being used against them by the Germans promoting their own racial purity".[[137]](#cite_note-145) However, the notebooks also contain instances of Heidegger writing critically of [biological racism](/wiki/Biological_racism "Biological racism") and biological oppression.[[138]](#cite_note-146)[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020-147)

A notable entry in the notebooks are his writings about his mentor and former friend [Edmund Husserl](/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl"), specifically relating to Husserl's Jewish heritage. In 1939, only a year after Husserl's death, Heidegger wrote in his *Black Notebooks*:

> the occasional increase in the power of Judaism is grounded in the fact that Western metaphysics, especially in
> its modern evolution, offered the point of attachment for the expansion of an otherwise empty rationality and calculative capacity, and these thereby created for themselves an abode in the "spirit" without ever being able, on their own, to grasp the concealed decisive domains. The more originary and inceptual the future decisions and questions become, all the more inaccessible will they remain to this 'race.' (Thus Husserl's step to the phenomenological attitude, taken in explicit opposition to psychological explanation and to the historiological calculation of opinions, will be of lasting importance—and yet this attitude never reaches into the domains of the essential decisions[....].)[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger2017[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidmwAvDwAAQBAJlpgPP1hlcspgPA67vonepageqffalse_67–68]-148)

For [Donatella Di Cesare](/wiki/Donatella_Di_Cesare "Donatella Di Cesare"), this seemed to imply that Heidegger considered Husserl to be philosophically limited by his Jewishness.[[141]](#cite_note-149) Recent research confirms the deep connection between Heidegger's philosophy and *[völkisch](/wiki/V%C3%B6lkisch_movement "Völkisch movement")* antisemitism in the *Black Notebooks*.[[21]](#cite_note-:1-23)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Heidegger married Elfride Petri on 21 March 1917 in a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") ceremony officiated by his friend [Engelbert Krebs](/w/index.php?title=Engelbert_Krebs&action=edit&redlink=1 "Engelbert Krebs (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Engelbert_Krebs "de:Engelbert Krebs")], and a week later in a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") ceremony in the presence of her parents.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger2009xxxvi-150) Their first son, Jörg, was born in 1919.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchalowDenker2010135-151) Elfride then gave birth to [Hermann](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_Heidegger&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hermann Heidegger (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hermann_Heidegger "de:Hermann Heidegger")] in August 1920. Heidegger knew that he was not Hermann's biological father, but raised him as his son. Hermann's biological father, who became godfather to his son, was family friend and doctor Friedel Caesar. Hermann was told of this at the age of 14; Hermann grew up to become a historian and would later serve as the executor of Heidegger's will.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECammannSoboczynski2014-152)

In the same year that he married his wife, Heidegger began a decades-long correspondence with her friend [Elisabeth Blochmann](/wiki/Elisabeth_Blochmann "Elisabeth Blochmann"). They were romantically involved in the summer of 1929.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESafranski199885–88,_108–81-153) Blochmann was [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish"), which raises questions in light of [Heidegger's later membership in the Nazi Party](/wiki/Martin_Heidegger_and_Nazism "Martin Heidegger and Nazism").[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood2019chapter_10-154)

In 1925, a 35-year-old Heidegger began what would be a four-year affair with Hannah Arendt, who was then 19 years old and his student. Like Blochmann, Arendt was Jewish. Heidegger and Arendt agreed to keep the details of the relationship a secret, preserving their letters, but keeping them unavailable.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoung-Bruehl200450-155)[[148]](#cite_note-156)

Heidegger had an active social life and interacted with numerous influential philosophers, authors, and academics. During his career he established relationships with [Karl Jaspers](/wiki/Karl_Jaspers "Karl Jaspers"),[[149]](#cite_note-157) [Ernst Jünger](/wiki/Ernst_J%C3%BCnger "Ernst Jünger"),[[150]](#cite_note-158) [Hans-Georg Gadamer](/wiki/Hans-Georg_Gadamer "Hans-Georg Gadamer"),[[151]](#cite_note-159) [Paul Tillich](/wiki/Paul_Tillich "Paul Tillich"),[[152]](#cite_note-160)[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiske1976-161) [Rudolf Bultmann](/wiki/Rudolf_Bultmann "Rudolf Bultmann"),[[154]](#cite_note-162) and [Hannah Arendt](/wiki/Hannah_Arendt "Hannah Arendt").[[155]](#cite_note-163)[[156]](#cite_note-164)[[157]](#cite_note-165) He corresponded with [Carl Schmitt](/wiki/Carl_Schmitt "Carl Schmitt") whom like Heidegger joined the Nazi party in 1933 and served together on the [Academy for German Law](/wiki/Academy_for_German_Law "Academy for German Law").[[158]](#cite_note-166)[[159]](#cite_note-167)

### Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Death")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Grab_Heidegger.JPG/250px-Grab_Heidegger.JPG)](/wiki/File:Grab_Heidegger.JPG)

Heidegger's grave in Meßkirch

Heidegger died on 26 May 1976 in [Freiburg](/wiki/Freiburg "Freiburg").[[1]](#cite_note-yrt1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-yrt2-2) A few months before his death, he met with Bernhard Welte, a Catholic priest, Freiburg University professor and earlier correspondent. Heidegger was buried in the Meßkirch cemetery.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELambert2007157–69-168)[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESafranski1998432-169)[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiske1976-161)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Influence")]

Heidegger is often considered to be among the most important and influential philosophers of the 20th century by many observers.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiske1976-161) American Philosopher [Richard Rorty](/wiki/Richard_Rorty "Richard Rorty") has ranked Heidegger as among the most important philosophers along with [John Dewey](/wiki/John_Dewey "John Dewey") and [Ludwig Wittgenstein](/wiki/Ludwig_Wittgenstein "Ludwig Wittgenstein").[[162]](#cite_note-170) [Simon Critchley](/wiki/Simon_Critchley "Simon Critchley") has praised Heidegger as the "most important and influential philosopher in the continental tradition in the 20th century".[[163]](#cite_note-171)

Slovenian philosopher [Slavoj Žižek](/wiki/Slavoj_%C5%BDi%C5%BEek "Slavoj Žižek") has referred to Heidegger as a "great philosopher" and rejected the notion that his alleged anti-semitism against him tainted his philosophy. While he acknowledges and criticizes the ways Nazism permeated Heidegger's work and his life, Žižek contends that these influences are neither essential to describing Heideggerian philosophy nor sufficient in dismantling it. Instead, Žižek finds that a blatant dismissal deprives opportunities to investigate the contradiction between Heidegger's principles and practices, as well as to "confront the uneasy questions he raised against such basic tenets of modernity as 'humanism', 'democracy', 'progress', etc." Žižek adds that the greatness he perceives in Heidegger's work is the extent to which its core ideas can survive as it is abstracted from historical context and reimagined in later eras.[[164]](#cite_note-172)

In France, there is a very long and particular history of reading and interpreting Heidegger's work. Because Heidegger's discussion of ontology is sometimes interpreted as rooted in an analysis of the mode of existence of individual human beings (Dasein), his work has often been associated with existentialism. Derrida sees [deconstruction](/wiki/Deconstruction "Deconstruction") is a tradition inherited via Heidegger (the French term "*déconstruction*" is a term coined to translate Heidegger's use of the words "*[Destruktion](/wiki/Destruktion "Destruktion")*"—literally "destruction"—and "*Abbau*"—more literally "de-building").[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZuckert1991-173) The influence of Heidegger on Sartre's 1943 *[Being and Nothingness](/wiki/Being_and_Nothingness "Being and Nothingness")* is marked. Heidegger himself, however, argued that Sartre had misread his work.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§3.1-99)[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidegger1998250–51-174)

[Hubert Dreyfus](/wiki/Hubert_Dreyfus "Hubert Dreyfus") introduced Heidegger's notion of "being-in-the-world" to research in [Artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence"). According to Dreyfus, long-standing research questions such as the [Frame problem](/wiki/Frame_problem "Frame problem") can be only dissolved within an Heideggerian framework.[[167]](#cite_note-175) Heidegger also profoundly influenced [Enactivism](/wiki/Enactivism "Enactivism") and [Situated robotics](/wiki/Situated_robotics "Situated robotics").

Some writers on Heidegger's work see possibilities within it for dialogue with traditions of thought outside of Western philosophy, particularly East Asian thinking.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMa2007-176)[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOldmeadow2004351–54-177) Despite perceived differences between Eastern and Western philosophy, some of Heidegger's later work, particularly "A Dialogue on Language between a Japanese and an Inquirer", does show an interest in initiating such a dialogue. Heidegger himself had contact with a number of leading Japanese intellectuals, including members of the [Kyoto School](/wiki/Kyoto_School "Kyoto School"), notably [Hajime Tanabe](/wiki/Hajime_Tanabe "Hajime Tanabe") and [Kuki Shūzō](/wiki/Kuki_Sh%C5%ABz%C5%8D "Kuki Shūzō"). The scholar Chang Chung-Yuan stated, "Heidegger is the only Western Philosopher who not only intellectually understands [Tao](/wiki/Tao "Tao"), but has intuitively experienced the essence of it as well."[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaozi20138-178) Philosopher Reinhard May sees great influence of Taoism and Japanese scholars in Heidegger's work, although this influence is not acknowledged by the author. He asserts it can be shown that Heidegger sometimes "appropriated wholesale and almost verbatim major ideas from the German translations of Daoist and Zen Buddhist classics." To this he adds, "This clandestine textual appropriation of non-Western spirituality, the extent of which has gone undiscovered for so long, seems quite unparalleled, with far-reaching implications for our future interpretation of Heidegger's work."[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMay1996xv-179)

Notable figures known to be influenced by Heidegger's work today include [Aleksandr Dugin](/wiki/Aleksandr_Dugin "Aleksandr Dugin"), a prominent Russian far-right political philosopher.[[172]](#cite_note-180)

### Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Criticism")]

According to Husserl, *Being and Time* claimed to deal with ontology, but only did so in the first few pages of the book. Having nothing further to contribute to an ontology independent of human existence, Heidegger changed the topic to Dasein. Whereas Heidegger argued that the question of human existence is central to the pursuit of the question of being, Husserl criticized this as reducing phenomenology to "philosophical anthropology" and offering an abstract and incorrect portrait of the human being.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHusserl1997-181) Aspects of his work have been criticized by those who acknowledge his influence. Some questions raised about Heidegger's philosophy include the priority of ontology, the status of animals, the nature of the religious, Heidegger's supposed neglect of ethics ([Emmanuel Levinas](/wiki/Emmanuel_Levinas "Emmanuel Levinas")), the body ([Maurice Merleau-Ponty](/wiki/Maurice_Merleau-Ponty "Maurice Merleau-Ponty")), sexual difference ([Luce Irigaray](/wiki/Luce_Irigaray "Luce Irigaray")), and space ([Peter Sloterdijk](/wiki/Peter_Sloterdijk "Peter Sloterdijk")).[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolland2018139–43-182)[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEElden201285–88-183) [A. J. Ayer](/wiki/A._J._Ayer "A. J. Ayer") objected that Heidegger proposed vast, overarching theories regarding existence that were completely unverifiable through empirical demonstration and logical analysis.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorner200090-184)

In 1929, the [neo-Kantian](/wiki/Neo-Kantian "Neo-Kantian") [Ernst Cassirer](/wiki/Ernst_Cassirer "Ernst Cassirer") and Heidegger engaged in [an influential debate](/wiki/Cassirer%E2%80%93Heidegger_debate "Cassirer–Heidegger debate"), during the Second [Davos Hochschulkurs](/wiki/Davos_Hochschulkurs "Davos Hochschulkurs") in [Davos](/wiki/Davos "Davos"), concerning the significance of [Kantian](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant") notions of freedom and rationality. Whereas Cassirer defended the role of rationality in Kant, Heidegger argued for the priority of the imagination.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENirenberg2011-185)The reception of Heidegger's philosophy by Anglo-American [analytic philosophy](/wiki/Analytic_philosophy "Analytic philosophy"), beginning with the [logical positivists](/wiki/Logical_positivism "Logical positivism"), was almost uniformly negative. [Rudolf Carnap](/wiki/Rudolf_Carnap "Rudolf Carnap") accused Heidegger of offering an "illusory" ontology, criticizing him for committing the fallacy of [reification](/wiki/Reification_(fallacy) "Reification (fallacy)") and for wrongly dismissing the logical treatment of language which, according to Carnap, can only lead to writing "nonsensical pseudo-propositions".[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarnap1931-186)[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarnap1966-187)

[Hegelian](/wiki/Georg_Wilhelm_Friedrich_Hegel "Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel")-[Marxist](/wiki/Marx "Marx") thinkers, especially [György Lukács](/wiki/Gy%C3%B6rgy_Luk%C3%A1cs "György Lukács") and the [Frankfurt School](/wiki/Frankfurt_School "Frankfurt School"), associated the style and content of Heidegger's thought with irrationalism and criticized its political implications. For instance, [Theodor Adorno](/wiki/Theodor_Adorno "Theodor Adorno") wrote an extended critique of the ideological character of Heidegger's early and later use of language in the *Jargon of Authenticity*, and [Jürgen Habermas](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Habermas "Jürgen Habermas") admonishes the influence of Heidegger on recent French philosophy in his polemic against "[postmodernism](/wiki/Postmodernism "Postmodernism")" in *[The Philosophical Discourse of Modernity](/wiki/The_Philosophical_Discourse_of_Modernity "The Philosophical Discourse of Modernity")*.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERockmore1992[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidfeWlC4ioGTYCnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA57redir_escyvonepageqffalse_57],_[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidfeWlC4ioGTYCnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA75redir_escyvonepageqffalse_75],_[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidfeWlC4ioGTYCnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA149redir_escyvonepageqffalse_149],_[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidfeWlC4ioGTYCnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA258redir_escyvonepageqffalse_258]-188)[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdorno1973-189)[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHabermas1990chapter_VI-190)

[Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell") considered Heidegger an [obscurantist](/wiki/Obscurantist "Obscurantist"), writing, "Highly eccentric in its terminology, his philosophy is extremely obscure. One cannot help suspecting that language is here running riot. An interesting point in his speculations is the insistence that nothingness is something positive. As with much else in Existentialism, this is a psychological observation made to pass for logic."[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussell1959[httpsarchiveorgdetailswisdomofwesthis00russpage303mode1up_303]-191) According to [Richard Polt](/wiki/Richard_Polt "Richard Polt"), this quote expresses the sentiments of many 20th-century analytic philosophers concerning Heidegger.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolt1999[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidpaxTAQAAQBAJnewbks1newbks_redir0printsecfrontcoverpgPA123hlenredir_escyvonepageqffalse_123]-192)

## In film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: In film")]

* *[Der Zauberer von Meßkirch](/wiki/Der_Zauberer_von_Me%C3%9Fkirch "Der Zauberer von Meßkirch")* (1989) is a German TV documentary about Heidegger, co-directed by Ulrich Boehm and [Rüdiger Safranski](/wiki/R%C3%BCdiger_Safranski "Rüdiger Safranski").[[185]](#cite_note-193)
* The film director [Terrence Malick](/wiki/Terrence_Malick "Terrence Malick") translated Heidegger's 1929 essay *Vom Wesen des Grundes* into English. It was published under the title *The Essence of Reasons* (1969). It is also frequently said of Malick that his cinema has Heideggerian sensibilities.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2003179–91-194)[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECavell1979xv-195)
* *[The Ister](/wiki/The_Ister_(film) "The Ister (film)")* (2004) is a film based on Heidegger's [1942 lecture course](/wiki/H%C3%B6lderlin%27s_Hymn_%22The_Ister%22 "Hölderlin's Hymn \"The Ister\"") on [Friedrich Hölderlin](/wiki/Friedrich_H%C3%B6lderlin "Friedrich Hölderlin"), and features [Jean-Luc Nancy](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Nancy "Jean-Luc Nancy"), [Philippe Lacoue-Labarthe](/wiki/Philippe_Lacoue-Labarthe "Philippe Lacoue-Labarthe"), Bernard Stiegler, and [Hans-Jürgen Syberberg](/wiki/Hans-J%C3%BCrgen_Syberberg "Hans-Jürgen Syberberg").
* *[Being in the World](/wiki/Being_in_the_World "Being in the World")* (2010) draws on Heidegger's work to explore what it means to be human in a technological age. A number of Heidegger scholars are interviewed, including [Hubert Dreyfus](/wiki/Hubert_Dreyfus "Hubert Dreyfus"), [Mark Wrathall](/wiki/Mark_Wrathall "Mark Wrathall"), [Albert Borgmann](/wiki/Albert_Borgmann "Albert Borgmann"), [John Haugeland](/wiki/John_Haugeland "John Haugeland"), and [Taylor Carman](/wiki/Taylor_Carman "Taylor Carman").

5. **[^](#cite_ref-78)** [Michael Inwood](/wiki/Michael_Inwood "Michael Inwood") provides a brief discussion of this term to illustrate Heidegger's use of language more generally: "The word 'care', which corresponds closely, if not exactly, to the German *Sorge*, has a range of senses. We can see this from the adjectives it forms and the words they contrast with: 'careworn' and 'carefree'; 'careful' and 'careless'; 'caring' and 'uncaring'. These oppositions are not the same: one can be, for example, both careworn and careless. In ordinary usage not everyone is careworn, careful and caring all the time. Some of us are carefree, careless or uncaring. Heidegger makes two innovations. First, he uses 'care' in a broad sense which underlies its diversification into the careworn, the careful and the caring. Second, in this sense of 'care', he insists, everyone cares; no one is wholly carefree, careless or uncaring. It is only because everyone is, in this fundamental sense, care-ful, that we can ever be carefree, careless or uncaring in the ordinary, or as he has it, the 'ontical', senses of these words. In the 'ontological' sense of 'care', everyone cares. All human beings, again, are 'ahead of themselves' (*sich vorweg*), roughly 'up to something' or on the look out for what to do. What about those mired in hopeless despair? Even those, Heidegger insists, are 'ahead of themselves': 'Hopelessness does not tear Dasein away from its possibilities; it is only a particular mode of being toward these possibilities' (BT, 236)."[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInwood19992–3-77)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-100)** "In a 1947 piece, in which Heidegger distances his views from Sartre's existentialism, he links the turn to his own failure to produce the missing divisions of *Being and Time* [i.e., "Time and Being"]. ... At root Heidegger's later philosophy shares the deep concerns of *Being and Time*, in that it is driven by the same preoccupation with [Being](/wiki/Being "Being") and our relationship with it that propelled the earlier work. ... [T]he later Heidegger does seem to think that his earlier focus on Dasein bears the stain of a [subjectivity](/wiki/Subjectivity "Subjectivity") that ultimately blocks the path to an understanding of Being. This is not to say that the later thinking turns away altogether from the project of [transcendental hermeneutic phenomenology](/wiki/Transcendental_hermeneutic_phenomenology "Transcendental hermeneutic phenomenology"). The project of illuminating the a priori conditions on the basis of which entities show up as intelligible to us is still at the heart of things."[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler2020§3.1-99)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-130)** See also [J. Habermas](/wiki/J._Habermas "J. Habermas"), "Martin Heidegger: on the publication of the lectures of 1935", in [Richard Wolin](/wiki/Richard_Wolin "Richard Wolin"), ed., *The Heidegger Controversy* ([MIT Press](/wiki/MIT_Press "MIT Press"), 1993). The controversial page of the 1935 manuscript is missing from the Heidegger Archives in [Marbach](/wiki/Marbach_am_Neckar "Marbach am Neckar"); however, Habermas's scholarship leaves little doubt about the original wording.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-142)** The 1966 interview published in 1976 after Heidegger's death as ["Only a God Can Save Us"](https://archive.org/stream/MartinHeidegger-DerSpiegelInterviewenglishTranslationonlyAGodCan/Heidegger-derSpiegelInterview1966). *[Der Spiegel](/wiki/Der_Spiegel "Der Spiegel")*. Translated by William J. Richardson. 31 May 1976. pp. 193–219. For critical readings, see the ["Special Feature on Heidegger and Nazism"](https://criticalinquiry.uchicago.edu/past_issues/issue/winter_1989_v15_n2/). *Critical Inquiry*. **15** (2) (Winter 1989 ed.). 1989. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1086/ci.15.2.1343581](https://doi.org/10.1086%2Fci.15.2.1343581), particularly the contributions by [Jürgen Habermas](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Habermas "Jürgen Habermas") and [Blanchot](/wiki/Maurice_Blanchot "Maurice Blanchot"). The issue includes partial translations of [Jacques Derrida](/wiki/Jacques_Derrida "Jacques Derrida")'s *Of Spirit* and [Philippe Lacoue-Labarthe](/wiki/Philippe_Lacoue-Labarthe "Philippe Lacoue-Labarthe")'s *Heidegger, Art, and Politics: the Fiction of the Political*.

### Works cited

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Works cited")]

For ease of reference, citations of *Being and Time* cite to the pagination of the standard German edition, which is included in the margins of both of the English translations, each of which has its virtues.

Heidegger's collected writings are published by [Vittorio Klostermann](/w/index.php?title=Vittorio_Klostermann&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vittorio Klostermann (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Verlag_Vittorio_Klostermann "de:Verlag Vittorio Klostermann")].[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgakisEnnis2015ix–xiii-196) The *[Heidegger Gesamtausgabe](/wiki/Heidegger_Gesamtausgabe "Heidegger Gesamtausgabe")* was begun during Heidegger's lifetime. He defined the order of publication and dictated that the principle of editing should be "ways not works". Publication has not yet been completed. The current executor of Martin Heidegger's Literary Estate is his grandson and a lawyer, Arnulf Heidegger.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerrmannAlfieri2021xv-197)

* Heidegger, Martin (1962). *Being and Time*. Translated by John Macquarrie and Edward Robinson. Harper Collins.
* Heidegger, Martin (1971a). "A Dialogue on Language between a Japanese and an Inquirer". *On the Way to Language*. Translated by Peter D. Hertz. Harper & Row.

### Archival collections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Heidegger&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Archival collections")]

* [Original Heidegger manuscripts](https://www.luc.edu/media/lucedu/archives/pdfs/heideggercollection.pdf) are kept at the [Loyola University Chicago](/wiki/Loyola_University_Chicago "Loyola University Chicago") archives. See also "The transcripts and photocopies of Martin Heidegger's writings were given to Barbara Fiand, SNDdeN, Ph.D., by Fritz Heidegger in 1978".
* [Martin Heidegger Collection, ca. 1918–1976](https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/680836900)
* [Guide to the Student Notes from Lectures by Martin Heidegger](https://oac.cdlib.org/findaid/ark:/13030/tf6z09p138/). Special Collections and Archives, The UC Irvine Libraries, Irvine, California.
* [Works by Heidegger and on Heidegger (categorization)](https://www.beyng.com/)
* [Publications by and about Martin Heidegger](https://nb-helveticat.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/search?query=any,contains,%22Martin+Heidegger%22&tab=LibraryCatalog&search_scope=MyInstitution&vid=41SNL_51_INST:helveticat&lang=de&offset=0) in the catalogue Helveticat of the [Swiss National Library](/wiki/Swiss_National_Library "Swiss National Library")
* Majority of Heidegger Archives. Online: [Deutsches Literaturarchiv](https://www.dla-marbach.de/) in the town of Marbach am Neckar, Germany. Also known as: [DLA – German Literature Archive](https://www.dla-marbach.de/). Most of Martin Heidegger's manuscripts are in the DLA's collection. Search for Heidegger in their [Manuscript collections is online here.](https://www.dla-marbach.de/katalog/handschriften/)
